steps.narration: In this lesson, we are going to answer one big question: What is a function? We will learn how to identify functions, use function notation, find domains, and work with piecewise definitions.

objectives: Understand the definition of a function
Use function notation to evaluate functions
Find the domain of a function

steps.narration: Think of a function like a machine. You feed it an input, it follows a rule, and it spits out exactly one output. So if the rule is x squared plus 1, you put in 3 and out comes 10. Put in negative 2, out comes 5. Zero goes in, 1 comes out. Every single input gives you exactly one answer. That is the key.

Here is the formal way to say it. A function from a set A to a set B is a rule that assigns to each element in A, exactly one element in B. A is called the domain, those are your inputs. B is called the codomain, those are your possible outputs. The phrase to remember: exactly one.

visual_data.definition_latex:
A rule from set $A$ to set $B$ that assigns
to each element in $A$ exactly one element in $B$.

$A$ = domain (inputs)
$B$ = codomain (outputs)
visual_data.definition_text:
A rule from set A to set B that assigns
to each element in A exactly one element in B.

A = domain (inputs)
B = codomain (outputs)

Key phrase: exactly one.

steps.narration: We can picture this with a mapping diagram. On the left we have the domain, on the right the range. Each element on the left gets exactly one arrow pointing to the right. Notice that a and c both map to 2. That is totally fine. Different inputs can share the same output. What matters is that each input has ONE arrow.

Let us test some rules. To each real number x, assign x squared plus 1. Is this a function? Yes. Every x gives exactly one output. Negative 3 maps to 10. Zero maps to 1. 3 also maps to 10. Two inputs landing on the same output is fine. One arrow per input. Function.

Now consider this. To each real number x, assign all numbers y where y squared equals x. So what about x equals 4? Well, 2 squared is 4, and negative 2 squared is also 4. That means 4 maps to BOTH 2 and negative 2. One input, two outputs. That breaks the rule. Not a function.

We can see this on a graph too. This is the curve y squared equals x, a sideways parabola. Watch what happens when we sweep a vertical line across it. It hits the curve in two places. Two y values for one x value. Fails the vertical line test. Not a function.

How about this real world example. To each person, assign their biological mother. Every person has exactly one biological mother. Alice has one mom. Bob has one mom. Carol has one mom. One arrow per person. This is a function.
Now flip it. To each mother, assign her children. Mom A has two kids, Alice and Carol. That is two outputs from one input. Not a function.
To each country, assign its capital. The United States maps to Washington D C. France maps to Paris. Japan maps to Tokyo. Each country has exactly one capital. Function.
Last one. To each U S state, assign its senators. Arizona has two senators. That is two outputs from one input. Not a function.
And that is the core idea of section 1 point 1. A function assigns exactly one output to each input. No more, no less. Next up, we will put this into practice with function notation and evaluation.
